The Python code accurately translates the Coq types, helper functions, and logic, including the integer division and binary string conversion.

The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification. The to_binary_p function correctly converts positive integers to binary strings using the same recursive logic as the Coq version (handling even/odd cases). The to_binary function adds the '0b' prefix and handles zero correctly. The rounded_avg_impl function returns NegativeOne when n > m, otherwise returns the binary representation of (n+m)//2, matching the Coq floor division semantics. The pre and spec functions correctly translate the Coq definitions.

Python mirrors the Coq functions: same n>m check, same floor division for (n+m)/2 matching Z.div, identical binary conversion (including 0 and negative handling), and pre/spec checks align via boolean equality.

Python mirrors the Coq implementation: returns NegativeOne if n>m, otherwise computes floor((n+m)/2), converts to binary with the same to_binary logic (including the error string for negatives), and uses matching result equality. Thus outputs match for all inputs.
